page: Alibaba Cloud ecs.se1ne.3xlarge is a memory-optimized virtual machine instance powered by the KVM hypervisor. It features 12 dedicated vCPUs from an Intel Xeon 8269CY processor running at 2.5 GHz, paired with 96.0 GB of RAM. This configuration delivers a high memory-to-vCPU ratio of 8.0 GB per core. The instance does not include local storage or GPU accelerators, and it provides a baseline network bandwidth of 2 Gbps.

Benchmark data indicates a clear tradeoff between single-core and multi-core performance. Single-core processing is relatively weak, with Geekbench single-core and single-threaded gzip compression scores falling into the bottom 10% to 25% of tested servers. However, multi-core performance is more robust, achieving average tier results in stress-ng multi-core tests, Geekbench multi-core benchmarks, and software compilation tasks. Memory bandwidth also performs in the average tier, with uncached read speeds of 59.2 GB/sec and write speeds of 49.35 GB/sec.

Given its high memory density and dedicated CPU allocation, the ecs.se1ne.3xlarge offers a cost-efficient profile for memory-bound workloads that do not require extreme single-thread performance. It is well-suited for database operations, Redis caching layers, and multi-threaded data processing. The lack of local storage requires pairing with external block storage, which should be factored into the overall infrastructure design.
description: Alibaba Cloud ecs.se1ne.3xlarge is a memory-optimized virtual server featuring 12 dedicated vCPUs powered by an Intel Xeon 8269CY processor at 2.5 GHz and 96.0 GB of RAM. This configuration provides a high memory-to-CPU ratio of 8.0 GB per core, making it suitable for memory-intensive workloads. The instance lacks local storage and GPU accelerators, relying on a baseline network bandwidth of 2 Gbps. Performance benchmarks reveal a tradeoff, with weak single-core capabilities but average multi-core and memory bandwidth performance. It is well-suited for database operations, Redis caching, and multi-threaded processing tasks where memory capacity is prioritized over raw single-threaded CPU speed.
